article.title: The State of AI, Product Marketing, and Tech Markets — March 7, 2026
article.content:
Today's newsletter digest reveals converging themes across product strategy, AI governance, and market dynamics. Here's what caught our attention.

Product Marketing's Evolution

Emily Kramer's MKT1 Newsletter emphasizes that product marketing is no longer a standalone function but a core component of product strategy. The framework for building PMM from scratch includes three pillars: market positioning that resonates with actual user pain points, messaging that translates features into outcomes, and go-to-market strategies that align with buyer journeys. This echoes advice from Lenny's Newsletter on the first 90 days in a new PM role—understand before you execute.

Apple's Silicon Strategy Deepens

Ben Thompson's analysis on Stratechery highlights how Apple's M-series chips represent more than performance gains—they're about controlling the roadmap. The new MacBook Neo fills the gap between Air and Pro, but the real story is vertical integration paying off in an era where generic processors can't match optimized silicon. This is the same playbook that made the iPhone successful.

AI's Governance Dilemma

The Anthropic controversy raises uncomfortable questions. Can AI companies remain neutral while serving government contracts? Thompson argues this isn't just about one company—it's about whether open-source models will fragment along geopolitical lines. The tension between technical neutrality and national security isn't going away.

Market Signals

Morning Brew's daily briefing points to Fed rate adjustments on the horizon, with tech earnings (especially AI-related companies) outperforming expectations. The S&P 500's 0.8% gain and Nasdaq's 1.2% rise suggest investor confidence in the sector, even as EV competition intensifies and traditional automakers accelerate their transitions.

Decision-Making Under Uncertainty

Shreyas Doshi's latest framework tackles a universal challenge: how do product leaders make high-quality decisions with incomplete information? His answer involves identifying "pseudo-problems" early and using influence leverage—minimum effort for maximum impact. This pairs well with the product interview prep guide from Lenny's, which covers everything from product design to execution.

Connecting the Dots

The throughline today: control and leverage. Apple controls its silicon roadmap. Anthropic faces pressure to control AI's societal impact. Product marketers must control the narrative between product and market. And all of this happens against a backdrop of markets trying to control uncertainty through information.
